description:
Goal: make `World` the sole owner of Compiler2's type universe. Signal: Compiler2 production code no longer calls `crate::types::new()` or stores old-world `crate::types::Ty`; instead, all Compiler2 type-bearing state and logic use the one `World`-owned `compiler2::types::Types` instance and its local `Ty` ids.

Strategy: add the local type store to `World`, expose crisp world-owned type operations, migrate Compiler2 structs/facts/artifacts/jobs from old-world `Ty` to local `compiler2::types::Ty`, and delete production `types::new()` calls rather than wrapping them.

Change:
- Add `types: compiler2::types::Types` to `World`.
- Add the minimal world surface needed to operate on the owned type store directly.
- Convert Compiler2 production structs that currently store `crate::types::Ty` to store `compiler2::types::Ty` instead.
  This includes activation keys, fact values, semantic summaries, and materialized artifacts.
- Rewrite Compiler2 production jobs and helpers to use the world-owned type store instead of minting fresh `types::new()` instances.
- Update Compiler2-focused tests and telemetry captures to observe the local type carrier.

Why:
- With interned ids, a type handle only has meaning inside the owning store.
- Fresh type universes in Compiler2 production code are a correctness bug waiting to happen, not a performance smell.
- Once `World` owns the one type store, types become just another compiler-owned identity domain.

Checks:
- Compiler2 production code has zero `crate::types::new()` calls.
- Compiler2 production code no longer imports old-world `crate::types::Ty`.
- Existing Compiler2 tests continue to pass with the local type carrier.
- New targeted tests prove activation-input joins and activation keys are computed within one shared type universe.
title: Compiler2 world: own one local type store and migrate all type-bearing state to it
